frameworks.name: population receptive field (pRF) mapping
frameworks.one_line: Voxel-wise receptive-field model fit to retinotopic visual responses.

frameworks.name: VASO-CBV fMRI
frameworks.one_line: CBV-weighted contrast used as a higher-specificity alternative to BOLD.

frameworks.name: BOLD fMRI
frameworks.one_line: Standard oxygenation-based contrast compared head-to-head with VASO-CBV.

core_question: Do BOLD and VASO-CBV fMRI yield different population receptive field properties in human visual cortex?
key_assumptions: VASO-CBV response is biased toward smaller vessels and thus more spatially specific.
pRF size estimates partly reflect vascular blurring, so reduced blurring should narrow them.
Simultaneous dual-readout acquisition lets BOLD and VASO be compared within voxel.
manipulations: Imaging contrast: BOLD vs VASO-CBV (simultaneous dual-readout 3D EPI at 7T).
Standard pRF mapping stimuli viewed during scanning.
Variance-explained matching between contrasts as a control.
key_findings: Eccentricity and polar-angle maps are similar across BOLD and VASO-CBV.
VASO-CBV has lower tSNR and variance explained than BOLD.
pRF size vs eccentricity slopes are similar across contrasts.
pRF sizes remain comparable even after equating variance explained.
interpretations: Vascular blurring does not dominate pRF size estimates in either contrast.
VASO-CBV does not yield narrower pRFs despite its expected higher spatial specificity.
limitations_noted: VASO-CBV exhibits lower tSNR and variance explained than BOLD.
